flashcards.front: What is the basic principle of Selection Sort?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion and moves it to the beginning of the array, building the sorted portion one element at a time.

flashcards.front: How many passes does Selection Sort require for an array of n elements?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort requires exactly (n-1) passes to sort an array of n elements since the last element automatically falls into place.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Selection Sort in worst, average, and best cases?
flashcards.back: All cases have O(n²) time complexity because the algorithm always performs nested iterations regardless of initial arrangement.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Selection Sort?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort has O(1) space complexity as it sorts in-place without requiring additional memory proportional to input size.

flashcards.front: Why is Selection Sort considered unstable?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort is unstable because it may change the relative order of elements with equal values when swapping the minimum element to its position.

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between selection sort and bubble sort in terms of swaps?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort makes at most n-1 swaps (one per pass), while Bubble Sort may perform many swaps in each pass, making Selection Sort better when swap cost is high.

flashcards.front: In Selection Sort, what does the inner loop do?
flashcards.back: The inner loop scans through the unsorted portion to find the index of the minimum (or maximum) element.

flashcards.front: What is the advantage of Selection Sort over other O(n²) algorithms?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort makes minimum number of writes/swaps (exactly n-1), making it valuable when memory writes are expensive, like in EEPROM or flash memory.

flashcards.front: Can Selection Sort be made to sort in descending order?
flashcards.back: Yes, by finding the maximum element instead of minimum in each pass, Selection Sort can sort in descending order.

flashcards.front: What is the recurrence relation for Selection Sort?
flashcards.back: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n), which solves to O(n²) using substitution or recursion tree method.

flashcards.front: Why does Selection Sort not benefit from early termination like Insertion Sort?
flashcards.back: Because even a fully sorted array requires the same number of comparisons to confirm sortedness, as the algorithm must still find the minimum in each pass.

flashcards.front: How does Selection Sort compare elements?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort uses comparison-based sorting, making at least n(n-1)/2 comparisons in total regardless of initial order.

flashcards.front: What is adaptive property in sorting algorithms?
flashcards.back: An adaptive algorithm's runtime improves with partially sorted input. Selection Sort is NOT adaptive as it always takes O(n²) time.

flashcards.front: For what value of n does Selection Sort outperform more complex algorithms?
flashcards.back: For small arrays (typically n < 50), Selection Sort often outperforms quicksort or merge sort due to lower overhead.

flashcards.front: What is the swap operation complexity in Selection Sort?
flashcards.back: Each pass performs exactly one swap (or zero if element already in correct position), so total swaps are at most n-1.
